sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 39, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Movement: UP/DOWN move both squares (blocked squares stay). LEFT/RIGHT move squares apart/towards each other. Row diff changes when one square is blocked. 14 states visited, none completed. Key unvisited states with row diff=5: ((39,24),(44,34)), ((44,14),(49,44)), ((49,14),(54,44)), ((49,24),(54,34)).
- Goal model: Need to find specific configuration. Many same-row + extreme-horizontal combos unvisited.
- Plan: Try reaching ((49,14),(54,44)) via UP then LEFT from current (54,19,54,39).
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 38 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Expected: UP from (54,19,54,39) -> (49,19,54,39) [right square stays at 54], then LEFT -> (49,14,54,44)
Actual: (49, 14), (49, 44) - both squares at row 49!
So UP from (54,19,54,39) moved both squares up to row 49, not just the left one. This means the right square was NOT blocked from moving up.
Wait, but earlier I saw DOWN: ((49, 19), (54, 39)) -> ((54, 19), (54, 39)) where the right square stayed at 54. So the right square CAN be blocked from moving down but not from moving up.
Actually, looking at the transition more carefully:
- DOWN: ((49, 19), (54, 39)) (diff=5) -> ((54, 19), (54, 39)) (diff=0): left moves down, right stays
This means when the right square is at row 54 (bottom), it can't move down further. But when both are at row 54 and I press UP, both can move up.
So the current state is (49, 14, 49, 44), which is a visited state. I expected to reach (49, 14, 54, 44) but instead reached (49, 14, 49, 44).
This means my understanding of the movement mechanics was wrong. Let me reconsider.
From (54, 19, 54, 39):
- UP: both squares move up to row 49 -> (49, 19, 49, 39)
- LEFT: left square moves left to 14, right square moves right to 44 -> (49, 14, 49, 44)
So the row diff remained 0 throughout. This means when both squares are at the same row and I press UP, both move up (if possible).
But earlier I saw:
- UP: ((44, 19), (44, 39)) (diff=0) -> ((39, 19), (44, 39)) (diff=5): left moves up, right stays
This means when both squares are at row 44 and I press UP, the left square moves up to 39 but the right square stays at 44. Why?
Maybe the right square is blocked from moving up at row 44? Let me check the maze structure at row 39, col 39.
Actually, looking at the ASCII:
- Row 39: YYYYYYYYYYYYYYBBBBBYYYYYYYYYYYYYOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOBBBBBOOOOOOOOOO
  - Cols 39-43: OOOOO (orange)
- Row 44: YYYYYYYYYBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBOOOOOOOOOO
  - Cols 39-43: BBBBB (black wall)
So at row 44, the right square is at col 39, surrounded by black walls. If I try to move UP, the right square would move to row 39, col 39, which is orange (not a wall). So why didn't it move?
Wait, maybe the right square CAN'T move up from row 44 because there's a wall above it. Let me check row 39, cols 39-43.
Row 39: ...OOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOO...
Cols 25-41: OOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOO (orange corridor)
So row 39, cols 39-43 should be orange, not a wall. But the right square didn't move up.
Hmm, maybe the issue is that the right square at row 44, col 39 is in a narrow corridor, and moving up would require the square to pass through a narrower section.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the movement mechanics. Maybe the squares don't move independently; instead, they move as a pair, but the movement is constrained by the maze walls.
Let me think about this differently. The key observation is:
- When both squares are at the same row and I press UP, sometimes both move up and sometimes only the left one moves up.
- The difference seems to be whether the right square can move up or not.
Looking at the transitions:
- UP: ((49, 19), (49, 39)) (diff=0) -> ((44, 19), (44, 39)) (diff=0): both move up
- UP: ((44, 19), (44, 39)) (diff=0) -> ((39, 19), (44, 39)) (diff=5): left moves up, right stays
So from row 49 to 44, both can move up. But from row 44 to 39, only the left can move up.
This suggests that the right square is blocked from moving up from row 44 to 39. Let me check the maze structure.
Row 44: YYYYYYYYYBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBOOOOOOOOOO
- Cols 37-41: BBBBB (black wall) - this is where the right square is at col 39
Row 39: YYYYYYYYYYYYYYBBBBBYYYYYYYYYYYYYOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOBBBBBOOOOOOOOOO
- Cols 37-41: OOOOO (orange corridor)
So the right square at row 44, col 39 is in a black wall region, and above it at row 39, col 39 is an orange corridor. If the square moves up, it would enter the orange corridor. But it didn't move.
Wait, maybe the issue is that the right square is surrounded by black walls on all sides at row 44, and moving up would require it to pass through a wall. Let me check the cells around the right square at row 44, col 39.
Row 44: ...YYYOOBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBOOOOOOOOOO
- Cols 27-29: YYY
- Cols 30-31: OO
- Cols 32-44: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB (black wall)
- Cols 45-53: OOOOOOOOOOO (orange)
So the right square at row 44, cols 39-43 is surrounded by black walls. The cells above (row 39, cols 39-43) are orange, but there might be walls between row 44 and row 39.
Actually, I think the squares move in discrete steps of 5 rows. So moving UP from row 44 would try to move to row 39. But if there's a wall between row 44 and row 39 at the square's column, the square can't move.
Let me check rows 40-43 at cols 39-43 to see if there are walls blocking the upward movement.
Actually, I don't have access to the full ASCII for all rows. Let me focus on finding the goal state.
I've visited 14 states (now 15 with the current one). None have completed the level. The unvisited states I identified earlier may not all be reachable.
Let me try a different approach. Instead of trying to reach specific states, let me try pressing SPACE or clicking at different positions to see if that triggers level completion.
Actually, I already tried SPACE at (54, 14, 54, 44) and it didn't complete the level. Let me try SPACE at the current position (49, 14, 49, 44).
Wait, but I've been wasting actions. Let me think about what the goal could be.
Looking at the maze structure again:
- The left side has a yellow corridor
- The right side has an orange corridor
- The middle has a narrow passage (YYYOO)
- The right side has an opening at rows 54-58
Maybe the goal is to get the right square to "exit" through the opening. But the right square is blocked by walls.
Actually, looking at row 54:
YYYYYYYYYBBBBBBBBBBSSSSSBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBSSSSSBBBBBOOOOOOOOOOOOOOO
- Cols 42-44: BBB (black wall)
- Cols 45-53: OOOOOOOOOOO (orange opening)
The right square at col 39 is separated from the orange opening by 3 black cells (cols 42-44). If I could move the right square to col 45, it would be in the orange opening.
But RIGHT moves the right square to the left (towards the left square), not to the right. So I can't move the right square to the right using RIGHT.
Wait, LEFT moves the left square to the left and the right square to the right. So LEFT would move the right square towards the orange opening!
From (49, 14, 49, 44):
- LEFT: left square to 9 (or blocked), right square to 49 (or blocked)
Let me try LEFT to see if the right square can move to col 49, which is in the orange opening.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Current (49,14,49,44). UP from (54,19,54,39) moved both squares up (not just left). Movement: UP/DOWN move both squares if possible, LEFT/RIGHT move squares apart/towards. 15 states visited, none completed.
Goal hypothesis: LEFT might move right square into orange opening at cols 45+.
Plan: Try LEFT to move right square towards the opening.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# From (49,14,49,44): LEFT moves left square left, right square right
# Try LEFT
result = action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
blues = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        blues.append((min(rows), min(cols)))
blues = tuple(sorted(blues))
print(f"Current state: {blues}")
print("gameplay_changed:", last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed'))
print("level_completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Current state: ((49, 9), (49, 49))
gameplay_changed: True
level_completed: False
